---
title: "Why MTTR Alone Misleads"
subtitle: "Add MOVA to Measure Real Risk"
author: "Caleb Kinney"
date: "BSides Charm 2026"
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
from datetime import datetime

import pandas as pd
import polars as pl
from great_tables import GT, loc, style
from plotnine import (
    aes,
    element_blank,
    element_line,
    element_rect,
    element_text,
    facet_wrap,
    geom_line,
    geom_point,
    ggplot,
    labs,
    scale_color_manual,
    scale_x_continuous,
    theme,
    theme_minimal,
)

metrics = pl.read_parquet("data/metrics.parquet")
summary = pl.read_parquet("data/summary.parquet")

STRATEGY_LABELS = {
    "oldest_first": "Oldest-First",
    "newest_first": "Newest-First",
}
METRIC_COLORS = {"MTTR": "#335C67", "MOVA": "#C94C1A"}
STRATEGY_COLORS = {
    "Oldest-First": "#1F6F78",
    "Newest-First": "#8B1E3F",
}
ROOM_BG = "#f5efe4"
PANEL_BG = "#fffaf2"
GRID_COLOR = "#d8cec0"


def fmt_days(value: float) -> str:
    return f"{value:.1f}"


metrics_labeled = metrics.with_columns(
    pl.col("strategy").replace(STRATEGY_LABELS).alias("strategy_label")
)

plot_metrics = (
    metrics_labeled
    .select(
        pl.col("strategy_label").alias("strategy"),
        "month_index",
        pl.col("mttr_days").alias("MTTR"),
        pl.col("mova_days").alias("MOVA"),
    )
    .unpivot(
        index=["strategy", "month_index"],
        on=["MTTR", "MOVA"],
        variable_name="metric",
        value_name="days",
    )
    .to_dicts()
)
plot_metrics = pd.DataFrame(plot_metrics)

newest_plot = plot_metrics[plot_metrics["strategy"] == "Newest-First"].copy()
oldest_plot = plot_metrics[plot_metrics["strategy"] == "Oldest-First"].copy()

tail_plot = (
    metrics_labeled
    .select(pl.col("strategy_label").alias("strategy"), "month_index", "aged_over_180")
    .to_dicts()
)
tail_plot = pd.DataFrame(tail_plot)

summary_display = (
    summary
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("strategy").replace(STRATEGY_LABELS).alias("Strategy"),
        pl.col("mttr_days").round(1).alias("MTTR (days)"),
        pl.col("mova_days").round(1).alias("MOVA (days)"),
        pl.col("aged_over_180").alias("180+ days"),
    )
    .select("Strategy", "MTTR (days)", "MOVA (days)", "open_count", "180+ days")
    .rename({"open_count": "Open"})
)

card_rows = (
    summary
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("strategy").replace(STRATEGY_LABELS).alias("strategy"),
        pl.col("mttr_days").round(1),
        pl.col("mova_days").round(1),
    )
    .sort("strategy", descending=True)
    .select("strategy", "mttr_days", "mova_days", "open_count", "aged_over_180")
    .to_dicts()
)

card_lookup = {row["strategy"]: row for row in card_rows}

shared_theme = (
    theme_minimal(base_size=22)
    + theme(
        figure_size=(11.0, 4.8),
        legend_position="top",
        legend_title=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major_x=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_line(color=GRID_COLOR, size=0.55),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill=PANEL_BG, color=None),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill=ROOM_BG, color=None),
        strip_background=element_rect(fill="#e9dfd0", color="#e9dfd0"),
        strip_text=element_text(weight="bold", color="#111827"),
        axis_title=element_text(color="#27343A"),
        axis_text=element_text(color="#27343A"),
        plot_title=element_text(weight="bold", color="#111827"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(color="#4b5563", margin={"b": 10}),
        legend_text=element_text(color="#27343A"),
        plot_margin=0.12,
    )
)

ojs_define(
    metrics_js=(
        metrics_labeled
        .with_columns(
            pl.col("month").dt.strftime("%Y-%m").alias("month_label"),
        )
        .select(
            pl.col("strategy_label").alias("strategy"),
            "month_index",
            "month_label",
            "mttr_days",
            "mova_days",
            "aged_over_180",
        )
        .to_dicts()
    ),
    summary_js=card_rows,
)
```

## Quick Question {.center .hook-slide}

::: {.metric-big .centered}
What would you do
if your MTTR doubled next month?
:::

::: notes
Pause here. Let them answer it silently before you move.
:::

---

## Most dashboards translate that into panic

::: {.incremental}
- Something slowed down
- The team got worse
- Leadership should worry
:::

::: notes
The audience already knows this reaction pattern. You are naming it so you can break it.
:::

---

## Here is the trap {.center .statement-slide}

::: {.metric-big .centered}
MTTR can rise
while risk drops
:::

::: {.kicker .centered}
Same team. Same capacity. Better prioritization.
:::

::: notes
Deliver this cleanly. Do not defend it yet.
:::

---

## This talk is really about metric design

::: {.lede}
MTTR is not a bad metric. It is a useful metric that is very easy to over-trust.
:::

::: {.callout}
Dashboards do not lie. They simply omit with confidence.
:::

::: notes
That dry line usually gets a small laugh. Then move directly into definitions.
:::

# Start With MTTR {.center .section-slide}

## MTTR is a flow metric

Time to remediate **closed** vulnerabilities.

::: notes
Emphasize closed. That word does most of the work.
:::

---

## MTTR answers one real question

::: {.metric-mid}
How long did the work we finished take?
:::

It is useful for spotting friction in completed remediation work.

---

## MTTR does not answer these

::: {.incremental}
- What is still open
- How old the open backlog is
- Whether exposure is shrinking right now
:::

::: notes
This is the limit. Not a flaw, a boundary.
:::

---

## Keep this sentence {.center}

::: {.metric-mid .centered}
MTTR measures completed work,
not current exposure.
:::

::: notes
This is one of the lines you want repeated back to you later.
:::

# Add MOVA {.center .section-slide}

## MOVA asks the missing question

::: {.metric-mid}
**Mean Open Vulnerability Age**
:::

How old is the risk we still carry?

---

## Flow and stock are different views

::: {.definition-grid}
<div class="definition-card">
  <div class="eyebrow">Flow</div>
  <h3>MTTR</h3>
  <p>How fast are we closing finished work?</p>
</div>
<div class="definition-card">
  <div class="eyebrow">Stock</div>
  <h3>MOVA</h3>
  <p>How old is the backlog we still own?</p>
</div>
:::

::: notes
Say flow and stock out loud. Security audiences recognize it immediately once named.
:::

---

## You need both

One tells you about throughput.

One tells you about exposure.

---

## Means can hide an ugly tail

95 findings are 7 days old.

5 findings are 400 days old.

The average looks civilized. The tail does not.

::: notes
Use a slightly dry tone on civilized.
:::

---

## So report the tail too

::: {.incremental}
- MOVA
- Open count
- 180+ day backlog
:::

# Same Data, Different Question {.center .section-slide}

## The data model is intentionally boring

```{python}
import polars as pl
from datetime import datetime

vulns = pl.DataFrame({
    "id": [101, 102, 103],
    "created_at": [
        datetime(2026, 1, 10),
        datetime(2026, 2, 5),
        datetime(2026, 3, 1),
    ],
    "resolved_at": [
        datetime(2026, 2, 1),
        None,
        None,
    ],
})
```

::: notes
The model is small on purpose: identifier, created date, resolved date.
:::

---

## Same data, first question {auto-animate="true"}

```{python}
mttr = (
    vulns
    .filter(pl.col("resolved_at").is_not_null())
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("resolved_at") - pl.col("created_at"))
        .dt.total_days()
        .alias("days_to_fix")
    )
    .select(pl.col("days_to_fix").mean().alias("mttr_days"))
)
```

::: notes
Closed only. Duration to closure. Mean. That is MTTR.
:::

---

## Same data, second question {auto-animate="true"}

```{python}
as_of = datetime(2026, 4, 1)

mova = (
    vulns
    .filter(pl.col("resolved_at").is_null())
    .with_columns(
        (pl.lit(as_of) - pl.col("created_at"))
        .dt.total_days()
        .alias("open_age_days")
    )
    .select(pl.col("open_age_days").mean().alias("mova_days"))
)
```

::: notes
Open only. Age as of now. Same schema, different filter, different truth.
:::

---

## The implementation point {.center}

::: {.metric-mid .centered}
This is not a new platform.

It is a different query.
:::

# Hold Capacity Constant {.center .section-slide}

## The simulation is deliberately boring

::: {.simulation-grid}
<div class="sim-card">
  <div class="sim-number">24</div>
  <div class="sim-label">months</div>
</div>
<div class="sim-card">
  <div class="sim-number">360</div>
  <div class="sim-label">starting backlog</div>
</div>
<div class="sim-card">
  <div class="sim-number">60</div>
  <div class="sim-label">new findings / month</div>
</div>
<div class="sim-card">
  <div class="sim-number">60</div>
  <div class="sim-label">fixes / month</div>
</div>
:::

::: notes
The point is to deny the audience escape hatches. Capacity does not change.
:::

---

## Almost nothing changes

::: {.incremental}
- Same arrivals
- Same fix capacity
- Same data model
- Same reporting window
:::

---

## One choice changes everything

::: {.metric-mid}
How the team picks the next 60 fixes.
:::

---

## Two prioritization strategies

| Strategy | What gets fixed first |
|---|---|
| Newest-First | most recently discovered findings |
| Oldest-First | longest-open findings |

::: {.muted}
One optimizes optics. One cleans the basement.
:::

::: notes
That line earns a small laugh if you do not oversell it.
:::

---

## The core loop is small enough to inspect

```{python}
def simulate(strategy: str) -> pl.DataFrame:
    for as_of in months:
        current = active_vulns(backlog, as_of)
        to_fix = select_to_fix(current, 60, strategy)
        backlog = resolve_vulns(backlog, to_fix["id"], as_of)
        rows.append({
            "mttr_days": compute_mttr(backlog, as_of),
            "mova_days": compute_mova(backlog, as_of),
        })
```

::: notes
Same arrivals, same fix rate, same backlog size. Only ordering changes.
:::

# Results {.center .section-slide}

## Newest-First makes MTTR look great

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-alt: "Line chart for newest-first prioritization. MTTR stays low while MOVA climbs sharply across 24 months."
(
    ggplot(newest_plot, aes(x="month_index", y="days", color="metric"))
    + geom_line(size=2.1)
    + geom_point(
        data=newest_plot[newest_plot["month_index"] == 24],
        size=3.2,
    )
    + scale_color_manual(values=METRIC_COLORS)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=[1, 6, 12, 18, 24])
    + labs(
        title="Newest-First",
        subtitle="Low MTTR, aging backlog",
        x="Month",
        y="Days",
    )
    + shared_theme
)
```

::: {.metric-mid .centered}
Low MTTR.

Old risk.
:::

::: notes
Tell them how to read it in one sentence: fast closures, terrible exposure.
:::

---

## Oldest-First makes MTTR look worse

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-alt: "Line chart for oldest-first prioritization. MTTR rises while MOVA drops and stabilizes at a much lower level."
(
    ggplot(oldest_plot, aes(x="month_index", y="days", color="metric"))
    + geom_line(size=2.1)
    + geom_point(
        data=oldest_plot[oldest_plot["month_index"] == 24],
        size=3.2,
    )
    + scale_color_manual(values=METRIC_COLORS)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=[1, 6, 12, 18, 24])
    + labs(
        title="Oldest-First",
        subtitle="Higher MTTR, younger backlog",
        x="Month",
        y="Days",
    )
    + shared_theme
)
```

::: {.metric-mid .centered}
Higher MTTR.

Lower exposure.
:::

::: notes
Make the contrast explicit. The bad-looking MTTR is the better risk outcome.
:::

---

## Same workload, opposite story

```{python}
#| echo: false
(
    GT(pd.DataFrame(summary_display.to_dicts()))
    .tab_header(
        title="After 24 months",
        subtitle="Same arrivals. Same fix capacity. Only prioritization changes.",
    )
    .tab_spanner(label="Flow", columns=["MTTR (days)"])
    .tab_spanner(label="Exposure", columns=["MOVA (days)", "Open", "180+ days"])
    .fmt_number(columns=["MTTR (days)", "MOVA (days)"], decimals=1)
    .data_color(columns=["MTTR (days)"], palette=["#E5F1F3", "#335C67"])
    .data_color(columns=["MOVA (days)", "180+ days"], palette=["#FFF1EA", "#8B1E3F"])
    .tab_style(
        style=style.fill(color="#f7f1e7"),
        locations=[loc.title(), loc.subtitle()],
    )
    .tab_source_note(source_note="MTTR alone rewards the wrong strategy.")
)
```

::: notes
This is your numeric confirmation slide. Do not read every cell.
:::

---

## The paradox is the point {.center .statement-slide}

::: {.metric-big .centered}
Higher MTTR.

Lower MOVA.
:::

::: {.kicker .centered}
Oldest-first looks slower while exposure actually shrinks.
:::

# Show The Tail {.center .section-slide}

## MTTR never shows the tail

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-alt: "Line chart comparing the count of open vulnerabilities older than 180 days for newest-first and oldest-first prioritization."
(
    ggplot(tail_plot, aes(x="month_index", y="aged_over_180", color="strategy"))
    + geom_line(size=2.2)
    + geom_point(
        data=tail_plot[tail_plot["month_index"] == 24],
        size=3.2,
    )
    + scale_color_manual(values=STRATEGY_COLORS)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=[1, 6, 12, 18, 24])
    + labs(
        title="180+ day backlog",
        subtitle="Newest-first keeps the tail alive",
        x="Month",
        y="Open vulnerabilities",
    )
    + shared_theme
)
```

::: notes
This is the backlog MTTR hides. Read the end state, then move on.
:::

---

## This is why the tail matters

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
oldest = card_lookup["Oldest-First"]
newest = card_lookup["Newest-First"]

print(
    f"""
<div class="score-grid tail-grid">
  <div class="score-card score-good">
    <div class="score-title">Oldest-First</div>
    <div class="score-metric">{oldest["aged_over_180"]}</div>
    <div class="score-label">findings older than 180 days</div>
  </div>
  <div class="score-card score-bad">
    <div class="score-title">Newest-First</div>
    <div class="score-metric">{newest["aged_over_180"]}</div>
    <div class="score-label">findings older than 180 days</div>
  </div>
</div>
"""
)
```

::: {.centered .muted}
Newest-first ends with every open item in the 180+ bucket.
:::

::: notes
Say the word every. It makes the cost obvious.
:::

# Reporting {.center .section-slide}

## If leadership sees MTTR alone {auto-animate="true"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
cards = []
for strategy in ["Newest-First", "Oldest-First"]:
    row = card_lookup[strategy]
    winner_class = " winner" if strategy == "Newest-First" else ""
    cards.append(
        f"""
        <div class="dashboard-card{winner_class}">
          <div class="dashboard-title">{strategy}</div>
          <div class="dashboard-value">{fmt_days(row["mttr_days"])} days</div>
          <div class="dashboard-label">MTTR</div>
        </div>
        """
    )

print('<div class="dashboard-grid">' + "".join(cards) + "</div>")
```

::: {.centered .muted}
This dashboard rewards the strategy that hides the backlog best.
:::

::: notes
Pause long enough for the room to see newest-first as the fake winner.
:::

---

## If leadership sees the system {auto-animate="true"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
cards = []
for strategy in ["Newest-First", "Oldest-First"]:
    row = card_lookup[strategy]
    card_class = "dashboard-card dashboard-detail"
    if strategy == "Oldest-First":
        card_class += " winner"
    cards.append(
        f"""
        <div class="{card_class}">
          <div class="dashboard-title">{strategy}</div>
          <div class="dashboard-stat">
            <span>MTTR</span>
            <strong>{fmt_days(row["mttr_days"])} days</strong>
          </div>
          <div class="dashboard-stat">
            <span>MOVA</span>
            <strong>{fmt_days(row["mova_days"])} days</strong>
          </div>
          <div class="dashboard-stat">
            <span>Open</span>
            <strong>{row["open_count"]}</strong>
          </div>
          <div class="dashboard-stat">
            <span>180+ days</span>
            <strong>{row["aged_over_180"]}</strong>
          </div>
        </div>
        """
    )

print('<div class="dashboard-grid">' + "".join(cards) + "</div>")
```

::: {.centered .muted}
MTTR is still there. It just stops being the only story.
:::

::: notes
This is the leadership reporting slide. Let the reveal do the work.
:::

---

## Report a small set of metrics

| Signal | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| MTTR | closure speed |
| MOVA | age of current exposure |
| Open count | backlog size |
| 180+ days | risk stuck in the tail |

---

## Read them together

::: {.incremental}
- MTTR rising + MOVA falling = backlog cleanup
- MTTR falling + MOVA rising = optics improving while exposure worsens
- 180+ rising = risk getting stranded in the tail
:::

::: notes
Treat metrics as diagnostics, not verdicts.
:::

# One Live View {.center .section-slide}

## Toggle the strategy

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof selected_strategy = Inputs.radio(
  ["Oldest-First", "Newest-First"],
  {label: "Prioritization strategy", value: "Oldest-First"}
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const row = summary_js.find((d) => d.strategy === selected_strategy)
  return md`**Month 24:** MTTR **${row.mttr_days.toFixed(1)}** days, MOVA **${row.mova_days.toFixed(1)}** days, **${row.aged_over_180}** findings older than 180 days.`
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const rows = metrics_js.filter((d) => d.strategy === selected_strategy)
  const data = [
    ...rows.map((d) => ({month: d.month_index, days: d.mttr_days, metric: "MTTR"})),
    ...rows.map((d) => ({month: d.month_index, days: d.mova_days, metric: "MOVA"})),
  ]

  return Plot.plot({
    width: 860,
    height: 320,
    marginTop: 20,
    marginRight: 30,
    color: {
      legend: true,
      domain: ["MTTR", "MOVA"],
      range: ["#335C67", "#C94C1A"],
    },
    x: {label: "Month", ticks: [1, 6, 12, 18, 24]},
    y: {label: "Days", grid: true},
    style: {
      background: "#fffaf2",
      color: "#182126",
      fontFamily: "Avenir Next, Franklin Gothic Medium, Trebuchet MS, sans-serif",
      fontSize: "14px",
    },
    marks: [
      Plot.lineY(data, {x: "month", y: "days", stroke: "metric", strokeWidth: 4}),
      Plot.dot(data.filter((d) => d.month === 24), {x: "month", y: "days", fill: "metric", r: 5}),
      Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#d8cec0"}),
    ],
  })
}
```

::: notes
Flip it once. This is a reinforcement slide, not a playground.
:::

# Close {.center .section-slide}

## MTTR alone can mislead {.center}

::: {.metric-big .centered}
MOVA shows whether
exposure is actually shrinking.
:::

---

## Final line {.center .statement-slide}

::: {.metric-big .centered}
If your metrics punish
the right behavior,

teams will optimize
the wrong one.
:::

::: notes
Pause after the last line and stop.
:::
